food_name: Cockles (Blanched Hawker Style) with Dark Sweet Soy Dipping Sauce

local_names.hindi: ब्लड कॉकल्स — उबले हुए हॉकर शैली
local_names.thai: หอยแครงลวกสไตล์หาบเร่

aliases: Kerang Rebus / Kerang Blanched (kerang = cockle/clam; also: siput)
หอยแครงลวกสไตล์หาบเร่

malay: Kerang Rebus / Kerang Blanched (kerang = cockle/clam; also: siput)

region_of_origin: Singapore and Peninsular Malaysia; cockles farmed in Indo-Pacific intertidal coastal zones (Selangor, Johor, Indonesia, Thailand)
food_subgroup: Blanched blood cockles (Tegillarca granosa) served hawker style with dark sweet soy, chili, and calamansi dipping sauce; composite dish record (cockle meat + sauce)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for blanched blood cockles hawker style as a composite dish. Closest parent concept: cooked blood cockle (Tegillarca granosa / Anadara granosa). FoodOn URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Blood cockle (Tegillarca granosa) blanched, served with dark sweet soy sauce and chili dipping sauce — composite hawker dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for this composite hawker dish. Closest parent: A0012 'Molluscs, bivalves, cooked'. Code A0012 used as closest parent match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific hawker dish preparation. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.description: 1 hawker plate (~160g: ~100g blanched cockles in shell drained = ~50g edible meat + ~40g dipping sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Singapore hawker plate of blanched cockles weighs approximately 160g, comprising roughly 100g of blanched cockles in shell (yielding ~50g edible meat) plus approximately 40g of dark sweet soy, chili, and calamansi dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) — Cockles / Kerang, blanched; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Mollusks, cockle, cooked (NDB 15157) for micronutrient cross-reference
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values represent the composite dish (blanched cockle meat + dipping sauce weighted at 50g meat : 40g sauce per 160g plate). Cockle meat macros anchored to HPB Foodo blanched cockle entry and USDA SR Legacy NDB 15157 (mollusks, cockle, cooked). Dipping sauce sodium contribution (~850mg Na per 40ml dark sweet soy) derived from HPB Foodo condiment entries for kicap manis. Composite per-100g values calculated by weighted average. Iron value (5.3mg/100g composite) is conservative relative to raw cockle meat (~12–22mg/100g meat) due to dilution by sauce component and shell weight basis.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of shellfish and condiment components — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for shellfish (GI ~0 for protein-dominant seafood) and dark sweet soy sauce (GI ~35–40 for sugar-containing soy condiments); composite GI estimated at ~35 (LOW)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for blanched blood cockles with dark sweet soy dipping sauce as a composite hawker dish. Cockle meat itself has negligible carbohydrate and a GI not applicable to the protein component. The carbohydrate contribution (~5g/100g composite) derives primarily from the dark sweet soy sauce (kicap manis). GI ~35 estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for soy-based sweet condiments and shellfish. Glycemic load per 160g hawker plate = ~3.0 (VERY LOW), calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (35 × 8g) / 100 ≈ 2.8, rounded to 3.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium from dark sweet soy dipping sauce (~782mg Na per 100g composite; ~1,250mg Na per full 160g hawker plate)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should request dipping sauce on the side and limit to 5–10 dips maximum, or substitute with chili and calamansi only (no dark soy), which reduces sodium by approximately 70%; the cockle meat itself is a low-sodium, high-potassium protein source and is otherwise suitable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load (~3 per hawker plate); high protein content supports postprandial glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Blanched cockles with dipping sauce carry a glycemic load of approximately 3 per hawker plate (VERY LOW), making this a suitable protein-rich hawker option for people with Type 2 Diabetes; the dark soy sauce contributes approximately 8g sugar per plate but does not materially elevate postprandial glucose at typical consumption volumes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium from dipping sauce is the primary cardiovascular concern; cockle meat provides beneficial omega-associated micronutrients (selenium 20mcg, zinc 2.5mg, vitamin B12 6mcg) and low saturated fat (0.3g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cockle meat component is cardioprotective (low saturated fat, high selenium and B12, cholesterol 45mg/100g composite), but the high sodium from dark sweet soy sauce (~1,250mg per plate) poses a meaningful cardiovascular risk; patients with CVD should consume cockles with minimal or no dark soy sauce and monitor total daily sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High iron (5.3mg/100g composite; ~12–22mg/100g cockle meat), zinc (2.5mg/100g), selenium (20mcg/100g), and vitamin B12 (6mcg/100g) are clinically relevant for RA patients with anaemia of chronic disease
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Blood cockles are among the highest dietary iron sources in Singapore hawker cuisine and are suitable for RA patients, particularly those with co-existing iron-deficiency anaemia or anaemia of chronic disease; zinc and selenium support immune modulation relevant to autoimmune conditions.

food_safety_immunocompromised.key_factor: Lightly blanched (not fully cooked) cockles carry risk of Hepatitis A virus (HAV) and Vibrio parahaemolyticus contamination
food_safety_immunocompromised.guidance: Immunocompromised patients, pregnant women, and elderly individuals should request fully cooked cockles rather than the standard lightly blanched hawker preparation, as brief scalding does not reliably eliminate HAV or Vibrio; this is a documented food safety concern in Singapore and Malaysia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blanched blood cockles (kerang) hawker style is a quintessential Singapore and Malaysian hawker dish featuring Tegillarca granosa (blood cockles / Anadara granosa), briefly scalded in boiling water and served with a dark sweet soy (kicap manis), chili, and calamansi dipping sauce. A standard hawker plate weighs approximately 160g: ~100g blanched cockles in shell (yielding ~50g edible meat) plus ~40g dipping sauce. The cockle meat is tender, slightly chewy, and has a distinctive metallic, iron-rich flavour due to the haemoglobin in the tissue — blood cockles are unusual among bivalves in containing true haemoglobin, giving them a characteristic reddish appearance when opened. Per 100g composite dish: ~75 kcal, 10g protein, 1.5g fat, 5g carbohydrate, 782mg sodium. Per 100g cockle MEAT: iron ~12–22mg (one of the highest dietary iron sources in Singapore hawker cuisine), zinc ~4mg, vitamin B12 ~6mcg. The dipping sauce dominates sodium: ~1,250mg per full plate. Glycemic load per plate is approximately 3 (VERY LOW). FOOD SAFETY NOTE: standard hawker blanching is brief and does not fully cook the cockle; immunocompromised patients should request fully cooked cockles due to Hepatitis A virus (HAV) and Vibrio parahaemolyticus risk. Commonly served as a standalone hawker snack, as a component of char kway teow, or alongside satay. Sources: HPB Singapore Foodo; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15157.

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo (primary regional source for Singapore hawker dishes) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 15157 (mollusks, cockle, cooked). Composite dish values calculated from weighted component proportions (50g cockle meat : 40g dipping sauce per 160g plate). Sodium value validated against HPB Foodo condiment entries for kicap manis. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (no direct measurement exists for this composite dish). Confidence is HIGH for macros and micronutrients; GI confidence is 0.9 (analogous estimate, well-supported).
notes: 1. COMPOSITE DISH RECORD: per_100g values represent the weighted composite of ~50g blanched cockle meat and ~40g dark sweet soy dipping sauce per 160g hawker plate, normalised to 100g. Raw cockle meat iron is substantially higher (~12–22mg/100g meat) than the composite value (5.3mg/100g) due to dilution by sauce and shell weight basis. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation for this composite hawker dish preparation is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture data for the composite dish (cockle meat + sauce) is not reported in available approved sources. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy for cooked cockle meat; sauce components do not contribute meaningful vitamin A. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for cooked molluscs; no trans fat sources in dipping sauce components. 4. FOOD SAFETY: standard hawker blanching (brief scalding) does not fully cook cockles. Hepatitis A virus (HAV) and Vibrio parahaemolyticus risk documented in Singapore and Malaysia. Immunocompromised patients, pregnant women, and elderly should request fully cooked cockles. 5. IRON NOTE: blood cockles (Tegillarca granosa) are named for their haemoglobin content — most bivalves have clear haemolymph — but blood cockles contain true haemoglobin, making them one of the richest dietary iron sources in Southeast Asian hawker cuisine. Per 100g edible meat: ~12–22mg iron. 6. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 7. Halal status: cockles are halal under standard Malay-Muslim dietary practice in Singapore and Malaysia; dark soy sauce used in hawker preparation is typically halal-certified.
